citation: Concepts follow standard GNSS ionospheric textbooks and ZGIIS operational practice (dual-frequency GPS, geometry-free TEC, DCB calibration, IPP mapping). For the full mathematical derivation see Calculating VTEC on this site.

computation_pipeline.output: VTEC(TECU) over Zimbabwe → dashboard heatmap & archive

steps.title: Standing at Harare CORS — The Signal Path
steps.body: Imagine you are standing at a Zimbabwe CORS station in Harare. A GPS satellite sits about 20,200 km above you. The radio signal cannot reach your receiver directly — it must travel through the ionosphere, a layer of free electrons between roughly 50 km and 1,000 km altitude. Those electrons bend and slow the signal. That delay is exactly what Total Electron Content (TEC) quantifies — and it is what ZGIIS measures every day from the CORS network.
steps.why: Every map pin, RTK survey, and aviation GNSS fix in Zimbabwe depends on signals crossing this same ionospheric shell. When the shell is disturbed, positioning errors grow.

variables.meaning: Continuously Operating Reference Station — Harare (HARA/ZINH) and sites nationwide
Plasma layer where free electrons affect GNSS radio waves
Typical GPS orbital altitude above Earth

illustration.caption: GPS at ~20,200 km — the signal crosses the ionosphere before Harare CORS.

steps.title: What Is TEC?
steps.body: TEC means Total Electron Content — simply: how many electrons sit between the satellite and your receiver along the signal path. Imagine every electron is a grain of sand stacked between the satellite and the antenna. TEC is the count of all those electrons in a column of 1 m² cross-section along the path. The more electrons, the larger the TEC. We express TEC in TECU where 1 TECU = 10¹⁶ electrons per square metre.
steps.why: TEC is a single number that tells you how much ionospheric “traffic” a GNSS signal had to pass through — without counting electrons one by one in the lab.

equations.caption: Standard unit for total electron content

variables.meaning: Total Electron Content along the line of sight
TEC unit — 10¹⁶ electrons m⁻²

illustration.caption: TEC counts every electron along the path — like grains of sand between satellite and receiver.

</svg>

steps.title: Why Do We Care?
steps.body: Electrons slow GNSS signals. More electrons → more delay → larger positioning error. During geomagnetic storms the ionosphere over Southern Africa can become turbulent: RTK becomes unstable, phones lose accuracy, aircraft GNSS approaches degrade, and scintillation can break carrier-phase lock. So TEC is not an abstract number — it measures how disturbed the ionosphere is right now.
steps.why: ZINGSA monitors TEC alongside Kp and Dst so farmers, surveyors, pilots, and citizens know when navigation will struggle even if the CORS caster still looks online.

variables.meaning: Extra travel time imposed by electrons — metres at GPS frequencies
Kp ≥ 5 or Dst ≤ −50 nT — ionosphere often shows elevated TEC and S4

illustration.caption: More electrons mean more delay and larger positioning errors during storms.

</svg>

steps.title: Why Two Frequencies? (L1 & L2)
steps.body: GNSS satellites transmit on at least two frequencies — GPS L1 at 1575.42 MHz and L2 at 1227.60 MHz. Electrons affect lower frequencies more than higher ones. Think of two cars through traffic: the slower car (L2) suffers more delay. Comparing L1 and L2 reveals how much electron “traffic” exists — that difference is the key to computing TEC without guessing the troposphere or clock errors.
steps.why: Dual-frequency receivers at every ZGIIS CORS site are what make real TEC measurement possible from live data — never from a single frequency alone.

variables.meaning: 1575.42 MHz — less ionospheric delay
1227.60 MHz — more ionospheric delay

illustration.caption: L2 slows more than L1 in the ionosphere — the difference reveals TEC.

</svg>

steps.title: The Appleton Equation — How Radio Meets Plasma
steps.body: The Appleton–Hartree equation describes how radio waves behave in a plasma — air filled with free electrons. The full form is complicated, but for GNSS it simplifies to η ≈ 1 − 40.3·Nₑ/f², where Nₑ is electron density and f is signal frequency. This single relationship is the foundation of GNSS ionospheric research: higher electron density means more bending and delay; higher frequency means less effect.
steps.why: This is why the same storm can disturb L2 more than L1, and why the L1−L2 difference isolates ionospheric delay in processing software.

equations.caption: First-order refractive index in the ionosphere (Nₑ in m⁻³, f in Hz)

variables.meaning: Refractive index — how much the medium slows the wave
Electron density (electrons per cubic metre)
Carrier frequency (Hz)

illustration.caption: The simplified Appleton equation links electron density and frequency to delay.
illustration.svg:
<svg class="vtec-illus-svg" viewBox="0 0 340 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid meet"><rect width="340" height="300" fill="#000000"/>

  <rect x="48" y="55" width="244" height="52" rx="8" fill="#111827" stroke="#a78bfa"/>
  <text x="170" y="88" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ffffff" font-size="14" font-weight="700" font-family="Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif">η ≈ 1 − 40.3·Nₑ/f²</text>

  <text x="90" y="168" fill="#ff4444" font-size="9" font-family="Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif">↑ Nₑ → more delay</text>

</svg>

steps.title: Electron Density vs TEC
steps.body: Electron density Nₑ is the number of electrons inside one cubic metre — like counting grains in one small box. TEC is different: it integrates electron density along the entire slant path from satellite to receiver. That integrated value is Slant TEC (STEC). You cannot convert a single density reading into TEC without knowing the full path length and profile through the ionosphere.
steps.why: Confusing density with TEC is a common mistake. ZGIIS products report STEC/VTEC — integrated quantities along each satellite ray.

equations.caption: Integral of electron density along the signal path

variables.meaning: Electron density — local, per m³
Slant TEC — integrated along the oblique ray

illustration.caption: Electron density is per m³; TEC integrates density along the entire signal path.

  <line x1="170" y1="130" x2="170" y2="198" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="170" y="216" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ffffff" font-size="9" font-family="Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif">∫ Ne ds along satellite → receiver</text>

</svg>

steps.title: From Slant TEC to Vertical TEC (VTEC)
steps.body: When the satellite is low on the horizon, the signal travels a long slant path through the ionosphere — STEC is large. Scientists usually want Vertical TEC (VTEC): the electron content through a vertical column, which is easier to compare between sites and times. A mapping function S(E) converts STEC to VTEC using the satellite elevation angle E. VTEC = STEC / S(E).
steps.why: The TEC heatmap and dashboard timelines on ZGIIS show VTEC at the ionospheric pierce point — the mapped vertical equivalent of each slant measurement.

equations.caption: Vertical TEC from slant TEC and elevation mapping function

variables.meaning: Mapping function — depends on satellite elevation E
Vertical TEC — standard product for maps and storm studies

illustration.caption: Slant TEC along the oblique path is mapped to vertical TEC for comparison.

</svg>

steps.title: Code vs Carrier Phase — and Geometry-Free TEC
steps.body: GNSS measures distance two ways. Code (pseudorange) is like shouting START…STOP and timing the pulse — accurate to roughly 30 cm–1 m. Carrier phase counts radio wave cycles like a fine ruler — accurate to about 2 mm, but with an unknown integer ambiguity (which wave did you start on?). Almost all TEC studies use carrier phase because its noise is tiny (±2 mm vs ±50 cm for code). The trick: subtract L1 minus L2. Distance, clocks, troposphere, and most errors cancel. What remains is TEC plus biases and noise — the geometry-free linear combination used in ZGIIS software.
steps.why: Without the geometry-free combination you cannot separate ionospheric delay from satellite clock and tropospheric errors in a single equation.

equations.caption: Geometry-free combination — standard in TEC software

variables.meaning: Pseudorange — robust but noisier (~decimetre to metre)
Phase observation — millimetre precision with ambiguity

illustration.caption: Code is noisy; carrier phase is precise — L1−L2 geometry-free isolates TEC.
illustration.svg:

  <text x="243" y="118" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ffffff" font-size="9" font-family="Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif">±2 mm (ambiguity)</text>
  <rect x="48" y="155" width="244" height="44" rx="6" fill="#111827" stroke="#ff8c00"/>

  <rect x="24" y="270" width="292" height="24" rx="5" fill="#111827" stroke="#244d73"/><text x="170" y="285" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ffffff" font-size="8" font-family="Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif">Carrier phase is precise; GF cancels most errors</text>
</svg>

steps.title: Biases, Calibration & Cycle Slips
steps.body: Even after L1−L2 subtraction, receiver hardware and satellite electronics add biases of several TECU — e.g. true TEC 20 TECU but measured 26 TECU without calibration. Satellite DCB files (CODE) and receiver DCB estimation remove these offsets. Phase ambiguity is an unknown integer number of waves at lock start; if the receiver loses lock (tree, cloud, scintillation, power cut) counting restarts — a cycle slip. Smooth TEC suddenly jumps. Severe ionospheric disturbance or solar flares can cause the same. ZGIIS processing must detect and repair slips before trusting the arc.
steps.why: Raw TEC is never published as-is on the dashboard — calibration and slip editing turn measurements into scientifically usable VTEC.

variables.meaning: Differential Code Bias — satellite and receiver hardware offsets
Sudden integer jump in carrier phase — must be detected and fixed

illustration.caption: Receiver and satellite biases must be removed; cycle slips look like sudden jumps.
illustration.svg:
<svg class="vtec-illus-svg" viewBox="0 0 340 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid meet"><rect width="340" height="300" fill="#000000"/>

  <rect x="48" y="102" width="244" height="32" rx="6" fill="#111827" stroke="#00ff88"/>

  <circle cx="200" cy="130" r="6" fill="#ff4444"/>
  <text x="210" y="128" fill="#ff4444" font-size="8" font-family="Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif">cycle slip</text>

</svg>

steps.title: Many Satellites → Zimbabwe Ionosphere Map
steps.body: At any moment GPS 1, 2, 3, 4… each give one slant TEC from Harare, Mutare, Bulawayo, and every CORS site. After mapping and calibration, those rays become a picture of VTEC over Zimbabwe — exactly what the ZGIIS dashboard heatmap, time series, and storm alerts display. When TEC rises during a geomagnetic storm, you are seeing the same electrons that disturb RTK, phones, and aviation navigation — now mapped in TECU over the country.
steps.why: This closes the loop: from one receiver looking at one satellite to a national ionospheric monitoring service for Zimbabwe.

variables.meaning: Ionospheric Pierce Point — where each VTEC value is geo-located
Live VTEC, S4, and storm context from the CORS network

illustration.caption: Many GPS satellites over Zimbabwe → the VTEC map on your dashboard.
